i SO It will be seen from above that the “ Political Guardian ”’ was not

‘ confined to one number, as stated by Mr. Byard on the authority of

W e the British Museum catalogue and Power’s “ List,” but that it reacheg

L SR ! at least to three. I am a good deal interested in J. B. Trotter. The

|._3 " = - best authority about him is, I think, the Editor of “Sinn Fein,” who

E S some time ago published an interesting account of him and some of

i his writings. E. R. Mc. C. DIX.

1}* Dear Sir,—With the exception of Ryan’s Irish Worthies, another |

‘ _ : work equally difficult to procure, Webb’s Compendium, is the only §

i “ ‘book of its kind relating to Ireland. A widespread feeling exists, ex |

! S - pressed by W. B. Doyle in your third number, that it is now inade- E

ViR quate as-a record of Irish intelligence and that the time has arrived |

i‘ "* . ~~ for a new edition. To bring out such an edition in a worthy manner g

:w S . several corrections of the present one ara necessary, more space too

: | should be allotted to many of the lives, and possibly less to many of ¢

* those connected in a kind of a way only with the country. Many

e characters of considerable eminence and repute whose lives are not

;;'ﬁ-’ already included and many who have “ crossed the bar™ since the

’* work appeared in 1878 should be introduced. The labour involved

lg,&,; : -would be enormous, but it would be lightened considerably if a page or ‘
SRR e two of the Irish Book Lover were devoted from: time to time to the 1
;"*ﬁ’i i correctwn of exlstmg errors and to standardlzmg and discussing the |
T S merits of those on whose behalf claims might be advanced for inclusion
R ¢ el ~ in a new edition. This would open the way for a more comprehensive

~and _authoritative work and whilst the additional volume of material
‘1o be dealt with would undoubtedly be-very considerable it is a ques',
-—*  tion’ for consideration whether the work, which no one can hope any.
‘ longer to see brought out in one convenient volume could not be madé
a greater success-—-at least, ﬁnanclally—by producing it in five volumes
*1 ' —one for each province and the other to be devoted to those celebrities
whp were connected with the country either by immediate ‘descents
oﬂce, resldence, professmn, trade or by their wntlngs. In a generﬂ:' |
'-"fi; éense provmcmhsm is an undesirable: element to introduce in a nationd:.
under ‘i_“king (as & new edltlon of Webb might well be deacnbed) but th‘;
89 Here suggested in additionto being a convenience from the loc8" “}
Y j dﬁa %:‘point of view would stlmulate a healthy nvalry betwee‘;;-_‘._

2 THE IRISH BOOK LOVER

QUERY.

TEE NatroN.—What would be the value of six volumes of the.
““ Nation > (October 15th, 1842—July 29th, 1848), clean and well bound
I should feel obliged for your kind reply.

Clonmel. R. O’R..
REPLIES.

These volumes comprise the best portion of the journal extending:

. as it does from the commencement—* this October fifteenth, eighteen

*forty-two,” as poor Clarence Mangan sang—until its suppression. It
is difficult to say what they would fetch in the market, as outside the
American buyer, or public libraries, there is not much demand for

‘long runs of folios. I should value them at £5, although in the com--

petition of the sale room they might fetch more. I cannot trace any
set- having been sold in recent years, but perhaps some correspondent

'can. a
.+ In reply to E. D.’s query in No. 4, I wish to state that Donnchadh

- x,.uadh Mac Con-mara (anglice Donough MacNamara the Red), migra-

t.ed from his native Co. Clare and became famous as a schoolmaster
and poet in Counties Cork  and Waterford. His best known Irish
poems are “ The Adventures of a Slave.of Adversity,”’ a narrative poem
descnbmg aa voyage to Amenca and ‘ The Fair Hills of Ireland.”

He also wrote a fine Latin Elegy on Tadhy Gaolach. Donnchadh Ruadh.

dxed at the great age oi95vears at Newtown, near Kilmacthomas on 6th:

October, 1810. His poems have been edited by 8. Hayes [O’ Daly,. o
Dublm, 1803], John Fleming [m “ Trisleabhar na Gaedhilge,” Dubhn,-__

1884-6], .Tomas Flannghaile [S. B. and W., Dublin, 1897], and by

B,lseard 0. Foghludha Gill, Dubhn, 1908.] Very interesting. partlculal'ﬂ i

ni the: poet’s life. are: pubhshed in those four editions, in J ohn O’Daly’8

= i”" £, P,oeta ‘and Poetry of Munster.” (Dublm, 1849), and in the i Watef_‘
T ., fOrd Archmolozmal Journal >’ (1907.) His first editor. i now better’

known as Standish- Hayes 0’Grady, LL Dy whose reminiscences of the’

l B FORTHCOMING BOOKS.

l' t 2 Messrs. Constable announce a new Life of Richard Brinsley
8 e Sheridan, by Walter Sichel in 2 vols., 8vo., 81s. 6d. It is drawn largely
§ 1 SEae from manuscript sources to which the author was the first to have
!"'_ l;“":_"_'-_‘fi,'-??;: . access, amongst others a diary of the beautiful Duchess of Devonshire
l"' ‘ and several letters from her to ““ Dear Dick ” or ‘“ Old Sherry,” as he
g,..,’ e was named by his friends or opponents, for enemies he had none—
| e = except himseli. .

: S "~ We await with feelings of lively expectation the opinion of Mr.
t" ~ . Percy Fitzgerald, the author of ‘The Lives of the Sheridans,” upon
} %o~ this new work. We remember with what gusto he fell upon the
y.s=e = - family biography prepared with a deal of toning down—by the late
: i* : = Fraser Rae at the suggestion of the late Lord Dufferin, and tore it to
(e~ shreds in his “ The Real Sheridan,” 1897.

:,,, 2 - Bya cunous coincidence Mr. Murray announces a life of Sheridan’s
ol R - grand-daughter, Caroline, Lady Maxwell of Keir, but better known as
;;,, s - the Hon. Mrs. Norton, whose poems on ““ Bingen on the Rhine >’ and

“The Arab’s Farewell to his Steed,” thrilled our childhood. As is
BeaEet known the late (dear me! what a lot of ‘“late,”” eheu! fugaces) George
1 ” " Meredith £ounded his novel of *“ Diana of the Crossways’ upon a story
: - that had long been privately circulated, viz.: that Mrs. Norton wheed-
